entry.title: Re: Simplified and Flexible SAP Concur Integration for Service Order Expense Posting
entry.content:
Hi,

Great overview!

Just a quick note: in most cases, no manual steps are needed to replicate master data objects (such as service order items) from S/4HANA to Concur, or to import transactional data (such as expense reports) from Concur into S/4HANA. Once master data replication is set up, a one-time manual replication is required to ensure that all relevant master data objects are transferred to Concur. After that, any newly created or updated master data objects will be picked up by scheduled jobs and synchronized with Concur automatically. Similarly, the feedback loop described in step 5 is typically handled by an automated job. Depending on how the job is scheduled, expense reports can be retrieved from Concur as frequently as every 10 minutes.

Best Regards, Andreas

entry.title: Account 402101 requires an account assignment relevant to cost accounting
entry.content:
Hi Team,

The business is facing an issue with Sales Order 582560563. The user is attempting to post a Goods Receipt (MIGO) against PO 4530995000, but is receiving the following error:

"Account 402101 requires an account assignment relevant to cost accounting."

The user has highlighted that out of the total quantity of 6, a GR for 5 units was successfully posted in 2024 without any issues. However, while attempting to post the GR for the remaining 1 unit, the above error is being triggered.

As a temporary test, we maintained a cost element assignment in OKB9, and the posting completed successfully without any error. However, this is not the recommended approach, and we would like to understand the root cause of the issue.

entry.title: Re: Asset not fully depreciating with Dep to day approach...
entry.content:
Kindly verify whether any scrap value, memo value, or cutoff value has been defined for the asset, as these parameters influence depreciation calculations and may result in a residual amount remaining on the asset after the end of its useful life.

For further details regarding scrap value, memo value, and cutoff value please refer to the following KBA:

3620966 - Differences between scrap value, memo value and cutoff value

For additional information on the "depreciation to the day" functionality, kindly refer to the following documentations:

1940343 - Depreciations to the day

3423823 - Depreciation to the day is set in the depreciation key - The calculation of the remaining useful life from the useful life is not clear

3501221 - Remaining useful life calculation for daily depreciation key (and 24 posting periods)

entry.title: FI Documents Missing an ODN? The ODN Insert Report Fixes That in One Run
entry.content:
If Official Document Numbering (ODN) has been activated, there may be Financial Accounting (FI) documents that were posted before ODN was introduced and therefore do not contain an ODN number. In addition, some FI-relevant posting processes may not currently be integrated with the standard ODN framework, such as postings originating from Flexible Real Estate Management (RE-FX) or other applications that create FI documents through interfaces not covered by ODN.

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT) addresses these scenarios by allowing an ODN value and legal timestamp to be added to FI documents that have already been posted. The report can be used for individual documents or for larger volumes through a mass upload process, regardless of the originating application or posting source.

Scope Note

Scope Note: This report applies to SAP S/4HANA (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. It ships as part of the H1/2026 FI ODN framework enhancements together with the credit-memo / reversal / BAdI functionality. Availability starts at 2025 FPS02 and is back-ported to earlier releases via SAP Note 3739128. A prerequisite chain of seven additional SAP Notes must be applied in a specific order — see the Note Sequence section below.

Main Insight

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report closes a real operational gap. Whenever an FI document exists in the ledger without a matching entry in table GSLOG_LFD — or with an entry whose ODN value is empty — you now have a governed way to fix it without touching the accounting document itself.

Two things happen in one shot:

  1. The report writes (or overwrites an empty value) the ODN value on the accounting document.
  2. It writes (or overwrites an empty value) the ODN legal timestamp.

Two ways to run it:

  • Single-document mode — enter one document number, one company code, one fiscal year, one ODN Type, one ODN Value and one ODN Country; simulate; then commit.
  • Mass-upload mode — provide a CSV file with many document keys and the target ODN values. SAP Note 3739128 ships the file template.

The same report handles both customer and supplier invoice scenarios in Financial Accounting, and it applies whether the document was posted through FB01, FB70, FB60 (if ODN Value was left empty), FV70 + FBV0 (parked → posted), or any other FI entry point.

Two guardrails protect the process:

  • Users must be assigned to authorization object LFD_ODNREP to run Simulation and Update.
  • Only one ODN country per document — either the company-code country or the reporting country.

Where You'll See It in the System

1. The Selection Screen — Choose Your Target

The report entry screen lets you enter a single document (company code + document number + fiscal year) or upload a file for mass processing. You also select whether you want to Simulate or Update:

2. The Result — Traceable, Row by Row

After the run, every processed document is listed with its outcome — inserted, overwritten (only for empty records), or skipped with a reason. This log is the audit trail you'll hand to your compliance team:

3. Verification in FB03 — The ODN Is Now on the Document

Open the accounting document in FB03 and the ODN value now appears exactly where it would have if it had been captured at posting time (for system availability see SAP Note 3716857)

4. Additional Fiscal Data Tab — The Full Picture

On the Additional Fiscal Data tab of the same document, you see the full triple: ODN country, ODN value, and ODN legal timestamp — all three now maintained:

Two Typical Scenarios

Example 1 — Post-Enablement Backfill: You posted an FI invoicing document via transaction FB01 (Post Document) before ODN was enabled for that company code. The document has no ODN entry in GSLOG_LFD at all. Run the report in single-document or mass-upload mode to insert both the ODN value and the ODN legal timestamp — no reversal, no re-post.

Example 2 — Parked Document Cleanup: You created a parked customer invoice via transaction FV70 (Park Customer Invoice) and later approved it with FBV0 (Post Parked Document). Run the report against that single document to write the correct number and timestamp in place.

The BAdI Is Included

An easy misconception: "if the report writes directly to the tables, my custom numbering rules won't apply." Wrong. The ODN Insert report calls the BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI on every processed document — the same BAdI that fires during FB70 / FB75 / FB60 / FB65 entry, during FB08 reversals, and during BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST calls.

That means:

  • If you've implemented country-specific numbering logic in the BAdI, it runs automatically.

📖See the BAdI Help Portal page for the method signature and implementation guidance.

Note Sequence — Apply These in Order

If you're on a release below 2025 FPS02, implement the following SAP Notes step-by-step, in the exact sequence shown. Skipping the order can break dependencies between the prerequisite objects:

  1. 3708326 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, BAdI (Manual Input Scenarios)
  2. 3729848 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Supplier Invoice Class III
  3. 3720120 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Customer Invoice Class III
  4. 3739126 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Update Service Class II
  5. 3739128 — FI ODN - ODN Insert for Accounting Documents (the report itself)
  6. 3776153 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Class for BTE Events
  7. 3742537 — FI ODN - BAdI in Accounting Transactions; BAPI and Reversal Enablers
  8. 3742475 — FI ODN - FI ODN functions available for transactions FB65 and FB75

Only after all eight notes are applied is the retrofit report fully wired to the rest of the FI ODN framework (BAdI, reversals, credit memos). If you're already on 2025 FPS02 or higher, the whole set is delivered with the release — no action needed.

Practical Takeaway

To adopt the ODN Insert report:

  • Confirm your release: 2025 FPS02 or newer, or an earlier release with the eight prerequisite SAP Notes applied in order
  • Run the customizing for your countries and processes in scope
  • Assign authorization object LFD_ODNREP to the users who will run Simulation and Update — and scope it to the ODN processes they're allowed to touch
  • Download the mass-upload file template attached to SAP Note 3739128
  • Run a Simulation first — on a single document, then on a representative mass-upload batch — and inspect the result list
  • Verify a handful of documents in FB03 (both the header and the Additional Fiscal Data tab) or via CDS view I_ACCOUNTINGDOCUMENTODN before scaling to production volumes
  • Coordinate the run with your compliance / audit team — the ODN legal timestamp is the value they'll ask about

Summary

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT) is the missing puzzle piece that lets you retrofit Official Document Numbers onto already-posted FI documents — either one at a time or thousands at once via mass upload (foreground processing: maximum 1,000 records; background processing: maximum 5,000 records). It calls the same BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI as every other FI ODN entry point, so your custom numbering logic applies automatically. Combined with the new FB08 reversal and FB65/FB75 credit-memo support, the FI ODN framework now covers a powerful scope.

Key Takeaways

  • Retrofit without reversal. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT writes ODN values and legal timestamps directly into already-posted FI documents — no reversal, no re-post, no modification of standard code.
  • Single or mass. Process one document interactively or upload a CSV file for bulk backfill; SAP Note 3739128 ships the file template.
  • The BAdI still fires. BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY is called during the retrofit run too — your country-specific numbering logic applies automatically, whether the document was posted yesterday or last year.
  • Authorization-controlled. Users need authorization object LFD_ODNREP to run Simulation and Update; scope it to the specific ODN processes they're allowed to touch.
  • One country per document. Either the company-code country or the reporting country.
  • Part of a bigger toolset. The retrofit report pairs with FB08 (reversals), FB65 (supplier credit memos), FB75 (customer credit memos), and the FI BAdI to give you complete FI ODN coverage.

Restrictions

  • The documents need to fit into the criteria to use one of the available ODN processes.
  • When the process is originated in logistics (cases of FI documents generated from sales or MM) this report inserts ODN only into the FI document.
  • Documents posted from modules for which there is not yet ODN support (such as Settlement Management) may fall back to FI ODN processes. It is advisable to use the report to cover these cases only if the ODN types can be customized for the FI processes consistently with the potential source processes.
  • In releases 103 and 102 there is currently no support for FB60 / FB65, FB70 / FB75, FB02 and FB03.
  • The reversal and BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST are enabled with simple ODN creation, meaning the ODN values are proposed initial, and the customer may adapt them using the BAdI.

More Information

  • Insertion of ODN in Accounting Documents — SAP Help Portal
  • BAdI: Modify ODN Value and Legal Timestamp for Scenarios in Financial Accounting (BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY) — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Notes (in application order): 3708326, 3729848, 3720120, 3739126, 3739128, 3776153, 3742537, 3742475

entry.title: Re: Account 402101 requires an account assignment relevan...
entry.content:
The system is consistent:

If a G/L account is a primary cost element, it requires a cost object. The fact that the first GR worked means the account determination or valuation setup was different at that time and subsequently, it has been changed. The error now is because the last GR is hitting 402101 without a cost object.

Do not rely on OKB9 defaults unless that’s truly the business rule. Instead, correct the account determination (OBYC) or material/PO account assignment so that free stock postings hit a proper expense account (secondary cost element or non‑cost element G/L). If 402101 must be used, ensure a valid cost object is assigned via PO account assignment category.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)
import cds from '@sap/cds'

export default class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
    async init() {

        const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

        // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
        // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
        // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
        this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
            // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
        })

        // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
        // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
        this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
            const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
            if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

            const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
            let newPosition = player.position + roll

            // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
            if (newPosition > 100) {
                await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
                const needed = 100 - player.position
                return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
            }

            // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
            const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
            let finalPosition = newPosition
            let event = null

            if (square?.ladderTo) {
                finalPosition = square.ladderTo
                event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
            } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
                finalPosition = square.snakeTo
                event = square.isDoubleHead
                    ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
                    : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
            }

            // Persist position and last roll
            await UPDATE(Players)
                .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
                .where({ ID: player.ID })

            // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
            if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

            // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
            // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
            // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
            req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
        })

        // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
        // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
        this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
            // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
            // For now: just let CAP apply @TO: #Waiting
        })

        // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
        // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
        this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
            // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
            // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
            req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
        })

        // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
        // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
        this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
            // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
            // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
        })

        // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
        // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
        this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
            const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
            if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

            if (player.position !== 100) return req.reject(400, 'Invalid Move! Player didnt finish')

            // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
            await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
                .set({
                    finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
                    winner_ID: player.ID
                })
                .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

            await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
        })

        // ── skipTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
        // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
        this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
            const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })

            // Increments the turnsBlocked counter on the player
            await UPDATE(Players)
                .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
                .where({ ID: player.ID })
        })

        return super.init()
    }
}​
  • A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state
  • A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder
  • One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it? 
    HTTP 400 error
  • Strech goal -  increments a turnsBlocked counter on the player

entry.title: Re: Make Optional Check number field for F-36 transaction
entry.content:
Hi,

In S/4HANA, SAP has enhanced Bill of Exchange and post‑dated check processing to enforce stronger compliance and traceability, treating each bill or check as a uniquely identifiable financial instrument. 
As a result, the Check No. field is mandatory by standard design in S/4HANA and cannot be switched off or changed via configuration. 
This is an expected functional difference between ECC and S/4HANA and is not a defect.

For more information, kindly check below SAP Help portal:
Posting Incoming Checks | SAP Help Portal 

Best regards,
Monika

entry.title: Re: [UPDATED] Cloud Identity Services Bug in SAP BTP Trial Environment
entry.content:
@Simmaco 

I've had similar problems before. Here's what helped me:


Delete the SAP Build Work Zone Standard Edition application and delete the trust config (sap.custom). Then clear your browser cache and log in again. Next, recreate the trust config for the CIS tenant, wait 15 minutes, and resubscribe to the SAP Build Work Zone Standard Edition application.

To delete the trust config, you must first delete all connections associated with it. (e.g. abap environment trial)

Check whether the Origin Key in the sap.custom trust config is set to OICD (OpenID Connect).

entry.title: Re: Production Confirmation defaulted as No Confirmation...
entry.content:
Inspection type 03 is linked to in‑process inspection during production order confirmation. When you trigger QA32, SAP checks whether the order has already been confirmed. The popup is designed to prevent accidental confirmations from QA transactions. So the system defaults to “No Confirmation” for safety. “Partial Confirmation” or “Final Confirmation” must be explicitly chosen by the user. The default behavior is not controlled by inspection type but by the confirmation profile in PP customizing. Profiles define whether partial or final confirmation is proposed.
If your business process requires “Partial Confirmation” as the default, you will need to adjust via enhancement.

entry.title: Teaching innovation in delivering SAP curricula and Engage industry in teaching SAP - Sri Lanka
entry.content:
It was an amazing opportunity to share our teaching experience with the SAP University Alliances community at the Teaching Seminar, held today as part of the SAP Academic Conference 2026 APAC.

For over a decade, the ERP Learning Hub at the University of Kelaniya, Sri Lanka, has been proud to grow alongside the SAP University Alliances programme. What began as an early academic partnership has evolved into a structured Diploma in ERP, delivered across three progressive stages, and complemented by seven certificate courses spanning the Functional, Technical (ABAP), and BASIS career pillars. This journey reflects the dedication of our team — Ms. Shanika Wijenayake (Director, ERP Learning Hub), Dr. Sugeeth Patabendige (Head, Department of Marketing Management), Ms. Isuri Udara (Coordinator, Diploma in ERP), and Mr. Dhuveeshan Muthusamy (Lecturer, Department of Marketing Management) — along with the continued support of the SAP UA community. We are happy to share our teaching innovation experience with fellow academics, and look forward to continuing this journey of embedding SAP technology into academic teaching and equipping students with real-world ERP skills. #SAPUniversityAlliances #ERPEducation #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Re: Query on Creating a Generic OData Connection in SAP A...

Hi @arjun_tko ,

 

Instead of using Generic OData connection, please leverage Import Data Connection to S/4HANA

for which you only need to provide https://<S/4HANA SERVER:PORT> for SAP S/4HANA On-Premise as Data Service URL.

In this case, the catalog service in SAP S/4HANA system will be responsible to provide a list of Odata Service and you could select one of them to create model instead of creating separate connection for each of them.

entry.title: Ariba Sourcing Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP – End-to-End Business Process
entry.content:
Introduction

Organizations often spend considerable effort managing supplier quotations, evaluating bids, and manually creating purchasing documents. By integrating SAP Ariba Sourcing with SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP, businesses can automate the complete sourcing lifecycle—from creating a Request for Quotation (RFQ) to automatically generating a Purchase Order after supplier award.

In this blog, we demonstrate the end-to-end business process using the Automation of Sourcing with SAP Ariba Sourcing (Scope Item 4BL). The process shown here represents a simplified version of the standard 4BL business process. For complete configuration and setup instructions, please refer to the official Scope Item 4BL documentation.

The blog is built as a series of 2 parts and present the key highlights for:

  • Ariba Sourcing Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP – End-to-End Business Process
  • Technical configuration for integrating Ariba Sourcing with SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP

Business Process Overview

The sourcing process begins in SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP, where a purchaser creates an RFQ. The RFQ is automatically transferred to SAP Ariba Sourcing, allowing strategic buyers to conduct the sourcing event, collect supplier responses, evaluate bids, and award the business to the selected supplier.

Once the supplier is awarded, SAP automatically synchronizes the result back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, where the corresponding purchasing document is created without manual intervention.

End-to-End Process Flow

Step 1: Create and Publish the RFQ

A purchaser creates a Request for Quotation (RFQ) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud using the External Sourcing Requirement option and publishes it.
This automatically creates a corresponding Sourcing Request in SAP Ariba Sourcing.

Step 2: Review and Approve the Sourcing Request

A Strategic Buyer logs into SAP Ariba Sourcing and:

  • Searches for the newly created sourcing request.
  • Completes the Prepare Sourcing Request task (if applicable).
  • Approves the sourcing request.
  • Opens the associated sourcing project.

This step ensures the sourcing request is ready for supplier collaboration.

Step 3: Publish the Sourcing Project

The Strategic Buyer reviews the sourcing project details and publishes the sourcing event, making it available for invited suppliers.
The sourcing event now becomes active for supplier participation.

Step 4: Supplier Participation

Suppliers log into SAP Ariba (or participate using a surrogate bid during testing) and perform the following activities:

  • Review the sourcing event.
  • Accept the participation terms.
  • Submit pricing and commercial responses.
  • Complete the bidding process.

Once submitted, the buyer can review all supplier quotations.

Step 5: Evaluate and Award the Supplier

The Strategic Buyer compares supplier responses and creates an award scenario.
After selecting the winning supplier, the sourcing event is awarded and the results are transferred back to SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Step 6: Automatic Purchase Order Creation

After the award is completed, SAP automatically creates the follow-on purchasing document in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Depending on the business configuration, this may include:

  • Supplier Quotation
  • Purchase Order
  • Purchase Contract (optional)

The purchaser can verify the generated document directly from the RFQ process flow.

 

Business Benefits

Integrating SAP Ariba Sourcing with SAP S/4HANA Cloud delivers several business advantages:

  • Eliminates manual transfer of RFQs between procurement systems.
  • Streamlines collaboration between buyers and suppliers through a unified sourcing process.
  • Accelerates supplier evaluation and award decisions.
  • Automatically creates follow-on purchasing documents, reducing manual effort and errors.
  • Improves procurement transparency with complete process traceability.
  • Standardizes sourcing activities using SAP Best Practice Scope Item 4BL.

Conclusion

SAP Ariba Sourcing and SAP S/4HANA Cloud work together to provide a seamless, end-to-end sourcing experience. Procurement teams can manage sourcing events efficiently in SAP Ariba while relying on SAP S/4HANA Cloud to automatically generate purchasing documents after supplier award.

By automating the sourcing lifecycle—from RFQ creation to Purchase Order generation—organizations can reduce manual effort, improve procurement efficiency, and enable faster supplier onboarding and purchasing decisions.

For more information:

https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-055/2025-FPS1/SolP/4BL?region=DE

entry.title: AddressLine1-8 formatting in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Form Templates
entry.content:
In our S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition implementation, we are currently performing customization development for form templates.
I would like to consult with you regarding the output of address information on these forms.

I understand that the address values for each form template are contained within the fields AddressLine1 through AddressLine8 in the XML.

Additionally, after checking the SAP Help site,

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/b86c68c2371c472796b6b54c9502ae97.html

I recognize that the output values and positions for AddressLine1 to AddressLine8 are determined by the following conditions, and that international/domestic addresses are output based on the address notation:

1. The length of the address is compressed based on priorities defined for each address and address type element.
2. Addresses are formatted based on current domestic and international guidelines:
・International address patterns of the Universal Postal Union (UPU)
・Regulations of each country
In this case, it is impossible to predict which AddressLine will contain the company name or specific address items.
For instance, if there is a customer requirement to make only the company name bold or increase its font size, or to separate the output positions of the company name and the address, we must identify the specific AddressLine for the company name.

Do you know any method to identify what value is stored in which AddressLine?
Also, if there is a way to achieve the above requirements using the AddressLine items, I would like to know.

Thank you.

entry.title: Batch characteristic to be transferred from outbound delivery to batch in Inbound delivery.
entry.content:
Requirement is to transfer one of the batch characteristics from Subcontract and Stock transfer Outbound delivery to new batch created in Inbound delivery.

Steps to reproduce:

  1. Create a Subcontract PO
  2. Create Outbound delivery (Batch with a characteristic maintained)
  3. Perform Post Goods Issue
  4. Create subcontracting challan
  5. Create Inbound delivery
  6. Maintain outbound delivery batch in inbound delivery "SC Components"
  7. Select line item and click on create batch
  8. Now we have to manually enter outbound delivery batch characteristic.

Requirement is to transfer one of the batch characteristics from Subcontract and Stock transfer Outbound delivery to new batch created in Inbound delivery.

Business want a batch characteristic called "Heat number" to be transferred from Raw material to FG product.

The "Heat number" characteristic value in Raw material's batch will be picked during outbound delivery creation (subcontract & STO process). The same characteristic value should be picked automatically in Inbound delivery.

entry.title: Re: What Are Authorizations?
entry.content:
1) Why are authorizations used? - SAP authorizations are used to ensure that users can access only the transactions, applications, functions, and data required for their job responsibilities.  With the autorization concept, you could control the level of access, mange risks and differentiate between organizational units within the same system.

For example, a user may be allowed to display vendor master data but not create or change vendors.

2) What are the elements of a role? 

The first tab is probably Role menu that you will see in transaction PFCG as soon as you open the desired role. The menu defines which applications are displayed to the user, for example: transactions, reports, fiori catalogs & spaces, URLs etc.

Next to Role menu you would find - Authorization data tab. Authorization data defines what the user is technically permitted to do. It consists of:

1. Authorization classes (collections of objects) that include authorization objects.

2. Authorization objects a unique element that define the logic and behaviour of a certain functionality.

3. Within each objects there are from one to many authorization fields that defines what action user is allowed to do (display, change, approve, etc.), or what type of document is allowed to use and so on. 

In addition there is a special type of authorization fields  that defines the depth to which user with assigned role is allowed to see a certain information. They called  - Organizational levels, such as company code, plant, purchasing organization, or sales organization.

Comnination of  authorizaiton classes, objects, fields and values is called an authorization profile that is generated and assigned to a role.

Finally you wuld see a User assignment tab. This is where you would see all the information regarding users assigned to a particular role.

3) What does SAP mean by role-based authorization concept?

A role-based authorization concept means that access is assigned according to a user’s business (position) role rather than by assigning individual permissions directly to each user.

A business role represents a collection of tasks performed by a particular job function, such as: Accounts Payable Accountant, Purchasing Specialist, Warehouse Operator, Sales Manager, SAP Basis Administrator etc.

4) Who already uses or is implementing this?

Role-based access control model ia a core model to be used in SAP and it is used by most medium-sized and large organizations operating SAP systems. It is commonly implemented in: SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA, SAP Business Technology Platform etc.
5) What advantages does the use of roles provide?

There are a lot of advantages of using the roles, but let's focus on a key ones:

Standardized access - Users performing the same business function receive a consistent set of permissions.

Simplified administration - Administrators assign one or more roles instead of maintaining individual authorization objects for every user.

Better security - Roles support least privilege by limiting access to what is required for a defined job function.

Improved auditability -  It is easier to explain why a user has access when the access is linked to a documented business role.

Better SoD control -  Roles can be analysed for conflicting activities before they are assigned to users.

6) Which steps are necessary for a user menu to be displayed for a user?

For a role menu to be displayed in SAP GUI, the following steps are normally required:

Administrator steps:

1) Create or maintain the role in transaction PFCG;
2) Add transactions, reports, applications, or other content to the role menu;
3) Maintain the role’s authorization data;
4) Generate the authorization profile;
5) Assign the user to the role on the User tab;

User steps:

1) A user logs off and logs on again or refreshes the SAP Easy Access menu.
2) A user selects the role-based menu.
In SAP GUI, this is normally displayed under the user menu rather than the complete SAP menu.

The user menu is primarily generated from the menu content of the assigned roles, while the ability to execute the displayed functions is controlled by the authorization profile.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud: A Look at 18 Months of Customer-Driven Deliveries
entry.content:
SAP Analytics Cloud's (SAC) Influence Campaign consistently attracts strong engagement from customers and SAP colleagues alike — a testament to the product's active user base and the high expectations that come with it.

In short, the SAC engineering team closed 167 influence requests representing a combined 4,089 customer votes across six release quarters through 2026 QRC2. This blog walks through what we built, why it matters, and where to learn more.

It's also worth noting that we delivered a significant number of additional features gathered through other channels, which are not covered here.

 

New Table Builder Experience

The brand-new Table Builder Panel brings the table design experience in line with that of Charts and Geo Visualizations, streamlining the story creation workflow. Beyond the builder itself, we delivered a range of enhancements covering sorting, ranking, data blending, parent and total positioning, and input readiness for planning scenarios.

 

 

New Table Builder Experience

 

Further reading:

The New Table Building Experience in SAP Analytics Cloud

New Table Builder Panel Becomes the Standard for Story Creation

The evolution of member ordering in SAP Analytics Cloud

Asymmetric Reporting Layout

Support of a Compact Display for Tables (Live BW)

Next Phase of the New Table Build Experience: Switching Existing Stories

Table Widget Improvements in SAP Analytics Cloud 2026 Q2 QRC

entry.title: Official Document Numbering Framework Extended: Credit Memos, Reversals, and a New BAdI
entry.content:
Hook

Until now, the Official Document Numbering (ODN) framework in SAP S/4HANA didn't cover credit memos or reversal documents in Financial Accounting. That has changed: the framework has been extended to support both scenarios natively, and a new BAdI gives you upgrade-safe control over ODN values across every FI entry point.

Scope Note

Scope Note: These enhancements apply to SAP S/4HANA (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Availability starts at 2025 FPS02, and the features are back-ported down to 2023 SPS06, 2022 SPS08, 2021 SPS10, 2020 SPS12, and 1909 SPS14 via SAP Notes 3739128, 3742537, and 3742475. If you're on one of the listed support packages or higher, you don't need to implement the notes — the functionality is already there.

Main Insight

The Official Document Numbering framework in SAP S/4HANA has been extended in three directions at once, and together they close gaps for Financial Accounting.

First, you can now maintain the ODN value and ODN legal timestamp directly on customer credit memos (transaction FB75) and supplier credit memos (transaction FB65) — the same way you already could on invoices. No more workarounds, no more manual post-fix reports.

Second, the same maintenance is available on document reversals. After the reversal is posted, the ODN value can be maintained via transaction FB02 on the reversal document.

Third, and most powerful for anyone with custom fiscal rules: a new BAdI — BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY — lets you programmatically override the ODN value and legal timestamp for any FI scenario. Derive it from another SAP document, pull it from an external tax portal, or apply your own numbering logic — all without modifying standard code.

Where You'll See It in the System

Credit Memos (FB65 / FB75)

The ODN and legal timestamp fields appear on the Additional Fiscal Data tab of the credit memo entry screen, exactly where you'd expect them alongside the invoice fields:

Document Reversals (FB08)

When you reverse an FI document, the ODN value can be displayed/maintained via transactions FB03/FB02 on the reversal document:

The BAdI: When Standard Isn't Enough

The BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI exposes a single method — CHANGE_USER_INPUT_ODN — that fires in every relevant FI scenario:

  • Customer Invoice (FB70) and Customer Credit Memo (FB75)
  • Supplier Invoice (FB60) and Supplier Credit Memo (FB65)
  • Change and Display Documents (FB02, FB03)
  • FI Document Reversal (FB08)
  • The new ODN Insert report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT), for overwriting empty records and creating new ones
  • BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST (so RFC and IDoc flows are covered too)

Because the same BAdI covers all entry points, you write your logic once and it applies consistently — user entry, batch inserts, and API-driven postings all take the same path.

🔦 Spotlight: Retrofitting Historical Documents

Retrofit is possible. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT handles historical accounting documents that were posted before ODN was enabled — or that are missing values today. It can both overwrite empty records and create fresh ODN entries, and it uses the same BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI, so any custom logic you already implemented applies to the retrofit run as well.

This is a topic in its own right — configuration, selection variants, dry-run vs. commit, and audit considerations. A dedicated deep-dive blog is now available.

📖 Read the deep dive: FI Documents Missing an ODN? The ODN Insert Report Fixes That in One Run →

Practical Takeaway

To adopt the enhancement:

  • Confirm your release: 2025 FPS02, or one of the listed support packages with SAP Notes 3739128, 3742537, 3742475 applied
  • Enable ODN for the accounting document types used for credit memos and reversals (same customizing path you used for invoices)
  • Test end-to-end in FB65 and FB75 — the fields should now be visible and editable
  • For reversals, this can be done via FB02
  • If you have custom fiscal numbering logic, implement BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY instead of user exits or modifications
  • For historical documents missing an ODN, run transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT to fill in the gaps

Summary

The Official Document Numbering framework in SAP S/4HANA now covers the full FI document lifecycle: invoices, credit memos, and reversals — with a new BAdI that gives you upgrade-safe control when your country's rules go beyond the standard.

Key Takeaways

  • Full coverage for credit memos. FB65 and FB75 now expose ODN and legal timestamp fields natively — no more custom workarounds for supplier and customer credit memos.
  • Reversals are no longer a blind spot. FB08 lets you enter a distinct ODN and legal timestamp for the reversal document, which is essential in sequential-numbering jurisdictions.
  • One BAdI, all entry points. BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY intercepts user entry, batch runs, and BAPI-driven postings — write your logic once, keep it upgrade-safe.
  • Retrofit is possible. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT handles historical accounting documents that were posted before ODN was enabled or that are missing values. (See the dedicated deep-dive blog for configuration, selection variants, and audit considerations.)

More Information

  • Maintain User Input Fields of Official Document Numbering Based On Additional Fiscal Data — SAP Help Portal
  • BAdI: Modify ODN Value and Legal Timestamp for Scenarios in Financial Accounting (BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY) — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Notes: 3739128, 3742537, 3742475

entry.title: Re: Porting Legacy Data Models in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Hi Klaus,

Thank you for the excellent blog post on importing legacy data models into SAP Datasphere. I found it very informative and it has sparked some interesting discussions within our architecture team.

I have a couple of questions that I was hoping you could clarify:

  1. The example in the blog uses SAP PowerDesigner to generate a CSN/JSON file for import into SAP Datasphere. Have you seen a similar approach successfully implemented using erwin Data Modeler? Specifically, can erwin generate a CSN/JSON format that is compatible with the SAP Datasphere import capability? 

  2. My understanding is that the imported model primarily covers entities, tables, relationships, and metadata structures within the Data Builder ER Modeler. Does the import capability also support the creation of Datasphere semantic objects such as:

    • Fact models
    • Dimension models
    • Analytical models
    • Business Builder objects

    or would these still need to be created manually within SAP Datasphere after the metadata import?

  3. In a scenario where SAP BW is the primary source system and Datasphere already consumes BW metadata, would you see CSN/JSON import as a replacement for native BW modelling, or more as a mechanism for migrating and governing logical/physical models?

I appreciate any guidance you can provide.

Kind regards,

Rajvinder Sahni

entry.title: Flexible Workflow Credit Memo Request – Unable to Upload Attachments Initial Save
entry.content:
We are experiencing a limitation with the Flexible Workflow for Credit Memo Requests in SAP.

Current Behavior

  1. During the creation of a Credit Memo Request, attachments cannot be uploaded before the document is saved and a document number is generated.
  2. Users can only add attachments after the initial save.
  3. Since the workflow is already triggered during document creation ( Approval state - 'To Be Reworked'), newly added attachments are not considered by the approvers unless the workflow is re-triggered.
  4. To re-trigger the workflow, users must make an additional change to the document (like changing the customer reference or date) so that a change log entry is written to the relevant standard tables and the workflow is triggered again.

Business Impact 

End users must perform multiple additional steps: Save the document. Upload the attachment. Modify the document again to trigger workflow re-evaluation. This process is time-consuming, inefficient, and leads to a poor user experience.

Expected Behavior

We would like to understand:

  • Whether it is possible to upload attachments during the initial creation of a Credit Memo Request before the first save and workflow trigger.
  • Whether adding an attachment after document creation can automatically trigger workflow restart/re-evaluation without requiring an additional document change.
  • If the current behavior is standard, are there any SAP-recommended configurations, enhancements, or best practices to address this scenario?

entry.title: Re: How to improve performance for federated data in SAP...
entry.content:
Since persisted data is not an option, I would recommend optimizing the virtual data model as much as possible.

  • Use SQL Views where feasible instead of multiple layered graphical views, as they generally provide better performance by pushing processing closer to the database engine and reducing unnecessary view stacking.
  • Implement parameters in Model to restrict data at runtime instead of loading the entire 13+ months of data. This helps reduce query volume and data transfer to SAC.
  • Reduce the number of exposed columns in the final consumption view and keep only the attributes and measures required by the SAC story.
  • Review complex joins and calculations (join cardinalities, outer joins, calculated columns, aggregations) and simplify them wherever possible to minimize runtime processing.
  • Run performance analysis in both SAP Datasphere and SAC to identify whether the bottleneck is related to query execution, data transfer, or story rendering.
  • Follow a lean consumption layer design by avoiding deep view hierarchies and exposing only the required data to SAC.
  • If possible, reduce the data volume at the source itself (if source is S4 e.g.,  at ABAP CDS View level) by applying filters, projections, and aggregations before the data reaches Datasphere.

entry.title: Designing JMS Queues for Scale in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Designing JMS Queues for Scale in SAP Integration Suite

The most difficult integration bottlenecks are the ones that remain invisible during the testing phase, and surfaces when production volumes increase.

In SAP Integration Suite, this scenario usually plays out around JMS queues. While JMS queues are the backbone of resilient, decoupled architectures, they run on a shared resource pool within your tenant, managed by SAP. Understanding these boundaries early during the design phase can turn production deployments from a guessing game into a predictable outcome.

In real implementation projects, JMS queue limitations usually become visible only when message volumes increase or downstream systems slow down. This article summarizes the design considerations I have found most important when my team is building and supporting JMS-based integrations in SAP Integration Suite.

 

SAP JMS - Core Capabilities

The JMS adapter in SAP Cloud Integration is the primary mechanism for asynchronous, event-driven integration patterns because it provides four core capabilities:

  • Asynchronous Decoupling: Senders drop payloads into the queue and immediately disconnect, protecting the source application from downstream performance bottlenecks.
  • Guaranteed Delivery: Messages are persisted in the queue until the consuming integration flow (iFlow) explicitly acknowledges successful processing.
  • Native Redelivery: Any processing rollback caused by an unhandled exception results in message redelivery according to the configured retry interval and exponential backoff settings.
  • First-In, First-Out (FIFO) Processing: Messages within a specific queue are processed sequentially in the exact order they were received. However, when multiple concurrent consumers are enabled using Non-Exclusive Access, messages are processed in parallel and completion order is no longer guaranteed.

Understanding the Resource Limits

A common architectural misconception is that JMS resource exhaustion is driven purely by open socket connections or message storage volume. In reality, SAP Cloud Integration enforces tenant-level quotas for JMS resources such as queues, concurrent consumers, concurrent transactions, and message storage. As more queues and consumers are deployed, these shared tenant limits are consumed, making efficient queue design essential for scalability.

Depending on tenant licensing and SAP provisioning, the JMS broker resource ceiling can reach the following maximum thresholds:

Individual JMS Queues: Default starts at 30 and scales up to 100 maximum.

Storage Capacity: Default starts at 9.3 GB and scales up to 30 GB maximum.

Concurrent JMS Transactions: Default starts at 150 and scales up to 500 maximum.

Concurrent Consumers: Default starts at 150 and scales up to 500 maximum.

The maximum number of consumers for one JMS Sender iFlow is calculated as:

Consumers = No of Runtime Nodes × No of Concurrent Processes

Runtime nodes are also referred to worker nodes.

 

Mathematical Sizing Scenarios

The impact of the Number of Concurrent Processes setting becomes much clearer when viewed against the maximum limit of 500 concurrent JMS consumers available in a tenant. The following examples illustrate how different runtime configurations influence resource consumption.

1. Baseline Configuration (Low Concurrency)

Configuration

Calculation

Result

2 Runtime Nodes, 1 Concurrent Process (default)

2 × 1

2 Consumers per iFlow

This configuration is highly efficient. In most landscapes, the 100 - queue limit is likely to be reached before the consumer limit.

2. High-Throughput Configuration

Configuration

Calculation

Result

2 Runtime Nodes, 10 Concurrent Processes

2 × 10

20 Consumers per iFlow

This single JMS iFlow consumes 20 of the available 500 consumers - 4% of the tenant's maximum consumer capacity. While this significantly improves throughput, it also reduces consumers available for other JMS-based integrations.

3. Large-Scale Deployment

Configuration

Calculation

Result

5 Runtime Nodes, 5 Concurrent Processes

5 × 5

25 Consumers per iFlow

If 20 integration flows are deployed with this identical configuration (20 × 25 = 500), the tenant reaches its maximum supported consumer limit. At that point, further JMS deployments or increased runtime demand may trigger resource allocation failures until capacity is freed or configuration is optimized.

The Retry Mechanism

When a message processing run encounters an unhandled exception, it is rolled back and flagged for redelivery based on the Exponential Backoff or Retry Interval configurations defined in the adapter. A few platform behaviors are critical to understand:

  • If an iFlow throws an unhandled exception, SAP retries that message indefinitely based on your configured Retry Interval and Exponential Backoff settings, there is no native "stop after N attempts" field.
  • To enforce a strict retry limit (e.g., stopping after 5 attempts), you must design an Exception Subprocess with a Router step evaluating: ${header.SAPJMSRetries} > '5'.
  • Setting the channel to Exclusive forces a single consumer thread to process messages strictly FIFO - a blocked retry pauses that entire queue.
  • Setting it to Non-Exclusive splits load across multiple concurrent processes, letting healthy messages bypass a single stuck retry, but ordering is no longer guaranteed.
  • Standard business or connectivity exceptions (e.g. HTTP 503) do not qualify for a native DLQ. A functional DLQ must be built by explicitly routing failed messages to a secondary custom JMS queue via an Exception Subprocess.

Production Guardrails Worth Building Into Every Design

To keep your integration flows with JMS queues stable, build integration patterns around these seven architectural realities.

1. Align Your Concurrent Processes to Target System Limits

A common pattern I have seen is teams increasing Concurrent Processes to clear a backlog faster, only to discover that the receiving system becomes the new bottleneck. Verify your backend can absorb that parallel load before ramping up concurrency, or you risk cascading database locks or HTTP 429 rate-limit errors that compound the original delay.

Example - Sales Orders into S/4HANA

[Online Store] → [JMS Queue: 10,000 orders backlogged] → [S/4HANA]

Scenario A — Concurrent Processes = 1:

  Single thread, ~2 orders/sec, clean but very slow (hours to clear).

Scenario B — Concurrent Processes raised to 50 to clear backlog faster:

  50 parallel threads hit S/4HANA at once.

  -> Database row locks on shared records (ERR_DB_LOCK_EXCEEDED)

  -> HTTP 429 Too Many Requests from the gateway

  -> All 50 messages roll back into the queue

  -> Exponential backoff pauses the whole queue

Result - Mass message rollback and exponential backoff clears the backlog SLOWER than with Scenario A.

2. Guard Against the Infinite Loop "Poison Pill"

SAP JMS queues have no built-in max retry limit for functional errors, by default they reprocess a failing message indefinitely. If a message contains a structurally broken payload and the queue is set to Exclusive Access for strict FIFO, that single "poison pill" sticks at the top of the queue and blocks every healthy transaction behind it until an engineer manually intervenes.

Example - Broken B2B Customer Onboarding

Requirement: strict FIFO (create must process before update).

Setting: Exclusive Access on the JMS Sender Adapter.

A partner's integration sends a malformed/empty XML payload:

  1. Consumer iFlow picks up the malformed message

  2. XML Validator / Mapping step throws a parsing exception

  3. Unhandled exception -> rollback -> message returns to queue head

  4. Retry timer expires -> same broken message handed back

  5. Steps 1-4 repeat indefinitely — no platform-level 'stop after N'

3. Build a custom Dead Letter Queue

The standard JMS retry mechanism in SAP Integration Suite primarily handles message redelivery for processing failures. It does not automatically separate application-level failures, such as mapping errors or business validation failures, into a dedicated dead-letter queue.

The Role of ${header.SAPJMSRetries} - During redelivery, SAP Cloud Integration provides the native header SAPJMSRetries, which indicates the current retry count.

Catching the error inside an Exception Subprocess allows you to read this header using a Router step to check the number of retries ${header.SAPJMSRetries}. If ${header.SAPJMSRetries} exceeds your defined threshold, route the payload to a dedicated Error Queue and complete the Exception Subprocess normally. This prevents the failed message from being redelivered indefinitely and allows subsequent messages to continue processing.

4. Set Alerting Thresholds

SAP Integration Suite enforces tenant-level JMS storage limits. As queue storage consumption approaches the configured tenant capacity, message producers may eventually be unable to persist new messages when the limit is reached. The exact warning thresholds and monitoring behaviour depend on the tenant configuration and monitoring tools in use. As an operational best practice, teams should define proactive alerts below the maximum capacity threshold (for example, 60–70%) to allow sufficient time to investigate growing backlogs and prevent storage exhaustion.

5. Segregate Batch Volume from Real-Time Traffic

Queues compete for processing threads across your runtime nodes. Never combine a heavy, low-priority bulk load (e.g. a nightly 50,000-record inventory sync) with time-sensitive, low-latency events (e.g. real-time customer checkouts) in the same queue, the batch load will saturate available consumer threads and bury critical messages for hours. Isolate these into separate dedicated queues, e.g. Queue_Inventory_Batch and Queue_Orders_Realtime.

6. Monitor Queue Depth, Not Just "Completed" Message Logs

A common operational blind spot is relying solely on Message Processing Logs (MPLs) to assess end-to-end message processing. When a producer iFlow successfully writes a message to a JMS queue, its MPL is marked as Completed, indicating that the message has been successfully handed over to the JMS broker. This status does not confirm that the message has been consumed or processed by a downstream JMS sender iFlow.

If the consumer iFlow is undeployed, experiencing repeated processing failures, or unable to keep up with incoming traffic, messages remain in the queue until they are successfully processed or otherwise handled. As a result, queue depth and storage utilization can continue to grow even though the producer iFlow reports successful completion.

For this reason, operational monitoring should include JMS-specific metrics such as queue depth, message age, and storage utilization alongside MPL status. Monitoring these indicators provides a more complete view of the health of asynchronous integrations and helps identify backlogs before they impact tenant capacity.

7.  The Multiplier Effect - Runtime Node Scaling

When fine-tuning high-volume queues, treating the Number of Concurrent Processes in the JMS sender adapter as a global tenant limit is a misconception. SAP Cloud Integration runs on a distributed architecture where your configuration acts as a per-node setting. The true connection footprint hitting your backend follows a strict mathematical relationship:

Total parallel threads = Active RunTimeNodes × Concurrent processes per queue

If your iFlow uses 5 Concurrent Processes and the Cloud Integration tenant runs on 2 runtime nodes, each runtime node can create up to 5 JMS consumers for that iFlow. This results in a potential total of 10 concurrent message-processing consumers across the runtime nodes. Therefore, your backend could receive up to 10 parallel requests (depending on message flow and processing time).

Since runtime node scaling is managed by SAP, your primary job as a developer is defensive design. Keep your Concurrent Processes configuration conservative (e.g - 2 to 5) and consider the total consumer calculation so that increased runtime capacity does not unintentionally create excessive parallel load on backend systems.

At the end of the day, JMS on SAP Integration Suite is only as resilient as the architecture and configuration built around it.

Disclaimer: This post is based on standard SAP documentation and real-world project experience, individual tenant limits may vary depending on your specific BTP subscription tier. All architectural formulas and metrics are for guidance purposes and must be verified against your specific SAP Global Cockpit configuration before deployment.

entry.title: Contractor/External Manpower Cannot Be Recorded During Operation Confirmation
entry.content:
Can any one suggest me the best solution:

Currently, during Work Order Operation Confirmation, only internal employees can be selected as personnel. Contractor or external manpower cannot be recorded, resulting in incomplete labor confirmations.

This causes:

  • Incorrect actual man-hour recording.
  • Inaccurate maintenance KPIs.
  • Incorrect labor utilization reporting.
  • Delays in Work Order closeout because actual effort cannot be fully confirmed.

Example

Operation Duration: 2 Hours

Resources Used:

  • 1 Internal Employee = 2 Hours
  • 1 Contractor = 2 Hours

Actual Total Labor = 4 Man-hours

Current SAP Confirmation:

  • Only 2 Hours (Internal Employee) can be recorded.
  • Contractor effort is missing.

entry.title: SAP BTP Cockpit Free Trial Config step

entry.title: From Process Design to Agentic AI with SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and Joule Studio
entry.content:
In our last post "Agentic AI Meets Academia: New No-Cost Offerings for Teaching", we introduced a set of no-cost agentic AI offerings available to academic institutions through SAP University Alliances. Today we go deeper — into the academic lesson itself, the scenario that ties all three tools together, and what this means for how you teach AI in 2026.

Agentic AI is no longer a concept reserved for research papers or hyperscaler demos. It is entering enterprise operations at scale. Students graduating today will be expected to understand not only what AI agents are, but how to analyze them, govern them, and build them. The question for educators is: how do you teach a technology that is genuinely new, spans disciplines from business informatics to computer science to ethics, and evolves faster than most curricula?

SAP University Alliances has developed an answer in the form of a structured, three-part Academic Lesson: From Process Design to Agentic AI with SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and Joule Studio. This lesson is designed for undergraduate and graduate students in business, computer science, information systems, and related disciplines — and it is free for institutions to adopt.

The lesson follows a deliberate arc: Analyze. Govern. Build. Each step builds on the previous one, and each step uses a different enterprise-grade tool. Together, they give students a comprehensive, end-to-end view of what it takes to deploy a responsible, effective AI agent in a real organization.

 

The Three Components

Each part of the lesson is anchored in a distinct tool and discipline, yet all three are interconnected by design.

Part 1 — Analyze with SAP Signavio

Students work with real agent trace data from an AI coffee shop scenario. Using SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, they import an event log, configure an analysis dashboard, and examine process variants, conformance gaps, and token consumption metrics. The activity introduces the concept of agent mining — applying process mining techniques not to human-driven workflows, but to the observable actions and handoffs of AI agents.

The key pedagogical insight: AI agents produce processes, and those processes can be mined, measured, and improved. Students who complete this part leave with the ability to treat an AI agent not as a black box, but as a process participant whose behavior is visible, comparable, and subject to continuous improvement.

The preparatory course on SAP Learning Hub is Analyzing Business Processes with SAP Signavio Solutions. Lecturers request a dedicated university tenant by contacting signavio.academics@sap.com. Three access models are available, including a no-cost, university-controlled tenant with the full SAP Signavio feature set. More information is available at SAP Signavio for Academic Institutions.

Part 2 — Govern with SAP LeanIX

Having analyzed the agents in process terms, students now ask a different set of questions: where do these agents sit in the enterprise IT landscape? What applications do they depend on? What AI-related risks do they carry? What governance obligations apply before they go live?

Students work in SAP LeanIX, activating the AI Governance and Adoption Extension, creating Application Fact Sheets for the four AI agents, assessing AI potential and risk across the company's application landscape, and building an initiative roadmap for deploying an AI chatbot into the company's claims management process. 

Preparatory resources include Putting AI Ethics into Practice at SAP and the on-demand live session AI Governance: How SAP LeanIX supports AI agents, both available on SAP Learning Hub, student edition. The SAP LeanIX Academic Edition is a free dedicated workspace for lecturers from accredited institutions. Contact leanix.academics@sap.com to request access, or visit sap.com/products/leanix.

Part 3 — Build with Joule Studio

The third part completes the arc. Students configure and deploy a working Joule agent in SAP Build that automates maintenance stock validation at the company. A maintenance planner asks the agent whether a specific order can proceed; the agent retrieves order details, identifies required materials, checks stock levels across five Joule skills, and returns a clear recommendation.

The preparatory course is Introducing Joule Studio in SAP Build, available on SAP Learning Hub, student edition. The hands-on build uses the SAP Build basic trial — a free, 30-day renewable environment that any student can register for independently at sap.com/products/technology-platform/build/trial. No institutional agreement, no procurement, and no technical setup by the lecturer is required. The Workbook "Create a Joule Agent with Joule Studio" is built into the trial environment and accessible to all registered users.

 

Why the Sequence Matters

Each part of the lesson can be completed independently, but the cumulative effect is what makes this lesson pedagogically distinctive. Students who complete all three parts can answer a question that neither process mining alone, nor architecture modeling alone, nor agent building alone can address: what does it actually take to deploy an AI agent responsibly in an enterprise context?

The process analysis in Part 1 reveals what the agents actually do — where they deviate from the intended process, how long each step takes, and where efficiency losses occur. That analysis directly informs Part 2, where students translate process insights into architecture decisions and governance classifications. The architecture blueprint from Part 2 provides the context that makes the build in Part 3 purposeful rather than procedural.

"Building an agent is only part of the job. Understanding process context, architectural placement, and governance implications is equally important — and those disciplines do not arrive naturally when you begin from the code."

 

What This Means for Lecturers

The lesson is designed with instructional flexibility at its core. You do not need to assign all three parts. Each part is self-contained and comes with complete lecturer notes, student-facing instructions, and possible deliverables. Whether you are teaching a single-session lab or a semester-length course module, the material can be adapted to fit your course design.

The suggested areas of study cover business, computer science, information systems, and AI. The lesson is suitable for both undergraduate and graduate students. No prior SAP experience is required for any of the three parts.

Access Requirements at a Glance

Tool

How to Access

Student Self-Registration

Cost

SAP Signavio

Lecturer contacts signavio.academics@sap.com for a dedicated university tenant

No — access granted through lecturer workspace

Free

SAP LeanIX

Lecturer contacts leanix.academics@sap.com for a free academic workspace

No — students invited by lecturer

Free

Joule Studio (SAP Build)

Students register at sap.com/products/technology-platform/build/trial

Yes — independent registration with SAP Universal ID

Free

SAP Learning Hub, student edition

Free for students and lecturers at accredited institutions via learning.sap.com/free-student-edition

Yes

Free

 

Download the Academic Lesson (V1) here:


MS Word format
PDF format

 

We look forward to hearing how you use this lesson in your classroom.

entry.title: Cost Center Automatically Reverts to Default Account Assignment and Cannot Be Changed Manually
entry.content:
Dear Community,

We are experiencing an issue in our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system regarding the determination of the Cost Center during journal entry posting.

Issue Description

When creating a journal entry, the Cost Center field is automatically populated from the Default Account Assignment.

However:

  • If we manually change the Cost Center to another valid Cost Center, the system immediately reverts it back to the default Cost Center.
  • If we remove the Default Cost Center from the Default Account Assignment configuration, the Cost Center field becomes non-editable (or the entered value is automatically cleared), preventing users from entering a Cost Center manually.

entry.title: Re: Closing Period Job Issue
entry.content:
Hi @Abdul2u 

 

Wether period closing on the 25th of the month is possible depends on the fiscal year variant assigned to the company code.

 

You can check the fiscal year variant in the configuration activity 'Maintain Fiscal Year Variants'

 

As @RaymondGiuseppi already mentioned there is checks performed as outlined in the KBA https://me.sap.com/notes/3094184/E 

for a period before it can be opened and that it is less than 10 days away

 

If the job is still failing and its setup as a recurring batch job then please review the KBA App Close Period for Product Master uses wrong time or date and ensure the batch job is setup correctly and that "Close Period" is set and not set "Check Period" or "Check and Close Period" 

 

Best Regards

Enda

Da dieser zu Ende geht, möchten wir parallel ein 8-stelliges, externes Nummernintervall für die gleiche Gruppe verwenden, um sowohl das alte interne als auch das neue externe Intervall für alle Auftragsarten nutzen zu können. In CO82 war es uns nicht möglich, diese Konstellation zu erstellen. Wir möchten wissen, ob es grundsätzlich möglich ist, und falls ja, wie wir diese Konstellation einrichten können.
